relations: The Brothers Grimm collected and published the tales included in Grimm's Fairy Tales

The Brothers Grimm are renowned for collecting and publishing the most well-known version of the fairytale "Snow White." This story, among others, has been attributed to their efforts in preserving folklore and bringing it to a wider audience. Their collection has played a pivotal role in the enduring popularity of "Snow White" and its place in cultural history.

The fairytale of "Hansel and Gretel" was collected and published by the Brothers Grimm. This story, which is among the numerous folktales that the Brothers Grimm brought together from various sources, has become one of their most famous and enduring tales.
The Brothers Grimm collected the fairytale of "Cinderella"
Jacob Grimm, as one of the Brothers Grimm, played a crucial role in the collection and publication of Grimm's Fairy Tales
Wilhelm Grimm, as one of the Brothers Grimm, played a crucial role in the collection and publication of Grimm's Fairy Tales
The story of Cinderella, including its ancient Greek version involving Rhodopis, has been told across different cultures

Charles Perrault is credited with popularizing the version of Cinderella that includes iconic elements such as the fairy godmother, glass slipper, and the pivotal stroke of midnight. He published this widely known version of Cinderella in 1697, thereby cementing its place in the annals of classic fairy tales. Through his work, Perrault has ensured that his rendition of Cinderella's story, with its magical transformations and themes of virtue rewarded, has become a beloved tale across generations.
Though Charles Perrault and the Snow White tale are part of fairy tale history, Perrault is not directly linked to Snow White but to other fairy tales like Cinderella
The Evil Queen is the stepmother of Snow White and the antagonist of the story
The Seven Dwarfs help Snow White escape her wicked stepmother
The Handsome Prince helps Snow White find her happily ever after
Hansel and Gretel outsmart the Cannibalistic Witch to escape
Jacob and Wilhelm Grimm were brothers who worked together to collect and publish fairytales
The tale of "Beauty and the Beast" has origins that can be traced back to Ovid's myth of Pygmalion and Galatea
The myth of Pygmalion and Galatea influenced the theme of love and transformation in "Beauty and the Beast"
